html:
SET:PRINC Thesis

Principal Capital is not the clean “asset-sale stub” story in the older draft. That was stale. The V Brilliant property disposal was a 2024 event, not an open 2026 catalyst: PRINC sold the property and hotel group for at least THB 5.942 billion, paid through THB 3.921 billion cash plus assumption of THB 2.021 billion of obligations, per the company’s transaction circular. The market has already had time to digest that. The stock is now down near multi-year lows because the remaining hospital business is still loss-making.

That is exactly why the thesis is more interesting, but also more conditional. PRINC is a post-disposal, pure healthcare network with 19 hospitals in 15 Thai provinces, depressed margins, heavy recent expansion cost, and almost no investor sponsorship. At the latest SET quote I found, the stock was THB 1.73, with a THB 6.59 billion market cap, 0.76x P/B, and tiny turnover of only THB 0.36 million on May 15, 2026. Foreign/NVDR ownership is negligible. This is not a crowded “Thai hospital” trade.

The variant view is that the market is valuing PRINC as a failed roll-up because the income statement still looks awful. FY2025 revenue rose 6.3% to THB 6.064 billion, but parent loss was THB 406 million, continuing operations lost THB 455 million, and gross margin slipped to 19.1% because doctor costs and direct costs rose faster than revenue. Interest-bearing debt also rose to THB 4.22 billion. On current EBITDA of only THB 464.5 million, the stock is not optically cheap.

But the forward setup is different. The company has already paid for much of the network. Q1 2026 revenue was THB 1.543 billion, up 5.5% in medical service revenue, while losses remained around THB 113 million attributable to shareholders. The question is whether the new hospitals and specialty centers can push the network from 7% to 8% EBITDA margin toward a low-to-mid-teens margin. If that happens, EBITDA power moves from roughly THB 0.45 billion to more than THB 1.1 billion without requiring heroic Thai healthcare multiples.

The upside torque comes from the fixed-cost nature of provincial hospitals. PRINC has been adding capacity before utilization. It acquired 57% of N.D.S. 34, which owns Por Phat 1 and Por Phat 2 in Nakhon Ratchasima, for about THB 674 million, adding long-established hospitals in a strong northeastern catchment. It is opening Pitsanuvej Kamphaeng Phet on June 1, 2026 after a roughly THB 600 million buildout, and it is layering in higher-value services such as fertility, elderly care, hemodialysis, physical therapy, and tertiary care at Suvarnabhumi. These are not commodity beds. They are attempts to build local referral density and case-mix complexity.

The stock’s mislabel is also important. PRINC used to be a hybrid property and hospital vehicle with related-party complexity. Now it is basically a healthcare operating platform, but investors still see the losses and debt rather than the network value. Book equity attributable to the parent was THB 8.73 billion at year-end 2025, versus a market cap under THB 6.6 billion. That book value is not a target by itself, but it says the equity is not pricing much success from a hospital network that is still growing revenue.

The biggest risk is that management keeps expanding before proving unit economics. Current ratio fell to 0.56x at FY2025, short-term liquidity became tighter, and interest coverage is still negative because EBIT is negative. This is not a “net cash downside protected” situation. If doctor costs stay elevated, new hospitals ramp slowly, or management funds more acquisitions with debt, the equity can keep leaking value. The related-party history also deserves a discount, even if the property exit itself simplified the story.

What I would underwrite is a 2 to 3 year operating leverage recovery, not an immediate re-rate. If revenue reaches THB 7.5 to 8.0 billion and EBITDA margin reaches 13% to 14%, PRINC can earn around THB 1.0 to 1.1 billion of EBITDA. At 12x EBITDA and assuming net debt around THB 3.0 to 3.5 billion after working-capital normalization, equity value is roughly THB 9.5 to 10.0 billion, or about THB 2.50 to THB 2.65 per share. That is not fantasy, but it requires proof.

The bull case is more powerful. If the newer hospitals mature, Suvarnabhumi becomes a credible specialty hub, fertility and elderly care attach to the network, and EBITDA margin reaches 16% to 17% on THB 8.5 to 9.0 billion revenue, EBITDA could approach THB 1.4 to 1.5 billion. A 13x to 14x hospital-network multiple would support THB 4.00 to THB 4.50 per share, even after debt. That is where the real upside is: not from the old property sale, but from a small hospital network moving from subscale losses into normalized private-hospital economics.

Bear case: THB 0.95 per share, down 45%. Revenue growth slows, gross margin stays below 20%, acquisitions fail to ramp, debt rises, and the market values the company at a distressed discount to book.

Base case: THB 2.55 per share, up 47%. FY2027 to FY2028 revenue reaches roughly THB 7.7 billion, EBITDA margin recovers to 13% to 14%, debt stabilizes, and the stock rerates to a still-discounted hospital multiple.

Bull case: THB 4.25 per share, up 146%. The new hospitals and specialty strategy work, EBITDA exceeds THB 1.4 billion, and PRINC is revalued as a scaled provincial healthcare platform rather than a broken roll-up.

At THB 1.73 today, PRINC is not a clean statistical bargain on current earnings. It is a neglected, illiquid turnaround where a small change in hospital margin can create a large change in equity value. The stock deserves a position only if one believes management is finally past the asset-shuffling phase and can prove operating discipline in 2026.
